name: spoke-cost
title: How do families pay for memory care in Skagit County?
body:
⚡ Quick answer
Most families combine several sources to pay for memory care — private savings and Social Security, proceeds from selling or renting the family home, long-term care insurance, Veterans benefits, and Medicaid (Washington Apple Health) . A monthly fee usually bundles a suite, meals, 24-hour dementia-trained care, a secured setting, and daily activities. Medicare does not pay for long-term memory care. And because the right figure depends on the level of care and room type, the honest answer to "what does it cost?" is a quick call — Whispering Willows accepts Medicaid without a spend-down .
On this page
What does memory care include?
What are the ways families pay for memory care?
Does Medicare pay for memory care?
Does Medicaid (Apple Health) cover memory care in Washington?
Does Whispering Willows accept Medicaid?
How do I get a cost estimate for my family?
If you've started looking into memory care for your mom or dad, the question of how to pay for it can feel like a wall — a worry that arrives with no context and no clear path. Take a breath. There are more ways to manage it than most families realize at the start, and you do not have to solve all of it today. Most families pay for memory care by combining several sources — private funds, insurance, Veterans benefits, and Medicaid (Washington Apple Health) — and the first, most reassuring step is simply understanding what you're paying for and the options for covering it.
This guide walks through what a monthly fee typically buys, the common ways families pay, the honest truth about what Medicare and Medicaid do and don't cover, and one point families here especially appreciate: Whispering Willows accepts Medicaid without requiring a spend-down . For a figure tailored to your loved one, the right answer is a short conversation — not a web price — and our Mount Vernon memory care team is glad to have it.
Key takeaways
Memory care is usually funded from several sources combined — private funds, home-sale proceeds, long-term care insurance, VA benefits, and Medicaid (Washington Apple Health).
Medicare does not pay for long-term custodial memory care, though it may cover short, medically necessary skilled care after a hospital stay.
Whispering Willows accepts Medicaid (Apple Health) — without a spend-down , so eligible families aren't required to first exhaust their assets to qualify for care here.
The right figure depends on the level of care and room type, so the honest answer to "what does it cost?" is a quick, personalized call.
What does memory care include?
Before the question of how to pay, it helps to understand what you're paying for — because the value of memory care is the whole package of safety, expertise, and daily life it provides. A memory care monthly fee typically bundles a generous set of services into one base figure, while certain needs are billed on top as they arise. The list below shows the general shape of what's usually included. This is industry structure, not a Whispering Willows fee sheet; every community draws the lines a little differently, so ask each one to walk you through its own.
What a monthly fee typically includes
A private or shared suite. A comfortable room your loved one can settle into and make their own.
Meals and snacks. Nutritious meals each day, with help and gentle prompting at the table as needed.
24-hour dementia-trained staffing. Around-the-clock care from a team trained specifically for memory loss.
A secured environment. A setting designed to keep residents who tend to wander safe, with freedom to move within it.
Housekeeping and laundry. Day-to-day upkeep so families can simply visit and enjoy their time together.
Life-enrichment activities. A daily rhythm of music, movement, and connection built around people living with dementia.
Basic personal-care assistance. Gentle help with bathing, dressing, grooming, and the everyday tasks of living.
When you compare communities, compare the all-in picture — what's included in the base fee and what may be billed separately as care needs change. That's also why a single advertised number rarely tells the whole story, and why a short conversation gives you a clearer answer than a web page can. If you're still weighing memory care against a less specialized setting, our guide to memory care vs. assisted living breaks down the difference.
What are the ways families pay for memory care?
Here is the reassuring part. Very few families write a single check from one account. Most assemble the monthly fee from a combination of the sources below — and some of these, like Veterans benefits, are missed simply because no one mentions them. Read these as options to explore, not advice; your own eligibility depends on your specific situation.
Common ways families pay
Private pay. Savings, pensions, Social Security, and retirement accounts — the most direct source for many families.
Proceeds from selling or renting the family home. Often the largest single source; selling frees the equity, while renting can create monthly income to offset care.
Long-term care insurance. If a policy is already in place, check whether memory care qualifies and review the elimination period (the waiting time before benefits begin).
Veterans benefits — VA Aid & Attendance. This pension can help eligible wartime veterans and their surviving spouses pay for care.
Medicaid — Washington Apple Health. For those who meet the income and asset limits, it can help cover long-term care. Whispering Willows accepts Medicaid without a spend-down.
Life insurance conversions. Some policies can be converted into a long-term-care benefit that pays toward care while your loved one is living.
Bridge loans or elder-care lines of credit. Short-term financing that covers care while a home sells or benefits begin, then is repaid once those funds arrive.
✓ A note on eligibility
Benefits programs each have their own rules, and they change. Before counting on any source above, verify your family's eligibility with the appropriate authority — the VA for Aid & Attendance, the Washington Health Care Authority for Apple Health (Medicaid), or a qualified benefits advisor or elder-law professional. Think of this as "talk to a professional," not financial advice — the right guide can often uncover help you didn't know you qualified for.
Does Medicare pay for memory care?
No — and this is the single most misunderstood point about paying for care, so let's be clear. Medicare does not cover long-term "custodial" memory care — the ongoing help with bathing, dressing, eating, and daily living that a residential memory care community provides. Medicare may cover short, medically necessary skilled care — for example, a limited skilled-nursing or rehabilitation stay after a hospitalization — but it does not pay for ongoing room-and-board memory care.
⚑ Important: Medicare does not pay for long-term memory care
Don't assume Medicare simply "covers" memory care — it doesn't cover the long-term, day-to-day custodial care that defines it. Medicare's role is limited to brief, skilled medical care, often after a hospital stay. Plan your funding around the other sources on this page, and let our team help you map the ones that fit your situation.
Does Medicaid (Apple Health) cover memory care in Washington?
Yes, for those who qualify. Washington's Medicaid program, called Apple Health , can help cover long-term care for people who meet the income and asset limits. Because eligibility rules and the availability of Medicaid beds vary by community — and not every community accepts Medicaid — the best way to know what applies to your family is to verify your situation with the Washington Health Care Authority or a benefits advisor, and to ask each community directly whether it accepts Medicaid. That last question matters, because the answer is not the same everywhere.
Does Whispering Willows accept Medicaid?
Yes — and we accept it without requiring a spend-down. That's a meaningful difference. At many communities, families are told they must first spend down their savings and assets to a low threshold before Medicaid will help. At Whispering Willows, eligible families are not required to first exhaust their assets to qualify for care here . It's part of how we try to make good memory care reachable for more families in the Skagit Valley — not just those who can private-pay indefinitely.
Every family's circumstances are different, and eligibility still depends on Apple Health's rules. The simplest next step is a short, no-pressure call: tell us a little about your loved one's situation, and we'll talk through eligibility and what it would mean for your family. There's no obligation — just a clearer picture.
The real question isn't only "what does it cost?" — it's "what are we buying?" For most families, the answer is safety, dignity, and the peace of mind of knowing someone they love is genuinely cared for.
— A guiding principle of person-centered memory care
How do I get a cost estimate for my family?
The honest answer is a quick conversation, not a number on a web page — and here's why. The right figure depends on the level of care your loved one needs and the room type you choose, and those vary from family to family. A single posted price would either over-promise or over-charge for someone; a short call lets us give you a figure that actually fits. Our Mount Vernon team will gladly walk you through a personalized quote , what's included, and the funding options — including Medicaid — that may apply to your situation.
When the numbers make sense, the companion guides below can help you spot the early signs that may have prompted this search, and choose a community you can trust. And whenever you're ready, a short visit or call will usually replace a frightening unknown with a workable plan.
Frequently asked questions
Does Medicare pay for memory care? +
No — not for long-term custodial memory care, which is the ongoing help with daily living that a residential community provides. Medicare may cover short, medically necessary skilled care, such as a limited skilled-nursing or rehabilitation stay after a hospitalization, but it does not cover ongoing room-and-board memory care. Families should plan to use other sources for long-term care.
Does Medicaid (Apple Health) cover memory care in Washington? +
Washington's Medicaid program, Apple Health, can help cover long-term care for people who meet the income and asset limits. Coverage and the availability of Medicaid beds vary by community, and not every community accepts Medicaid. The best way to know what applies to your family is to verify eligibility with the Washington Health Care Authority or a benefits advisor, and to ask each community whether it accepts Medicaid.
Does Whispering Willows accept Medicaid? +
Yes. Whispering Willows accepts Medicaid (Washington Apple Health), and we accept it without requiring a spend-down — meaning eligible families are not required to first exhaust their assets to qualify for care here. Because every family's situation is different, we recommend a short call so we can talk through eligibility and what it means for your loved one.
What does the monthly fee include? +
A memory care monthly fee typically bundles a private or shared suite, meals and snacks, 24-hour dementia-trained staffing, a secured environment designed to prevent wandering, housekeeping and laundry, life-enrichment activities, and basic personal-care assistance. Exact inclusions vary by community, so ask each one to walk you through what's included in the base fee and what may be billed separately as needs change.
How do I get a price for my family? +
The honest answer is a quick conversation rather than a number on a web page, because the right figure depends on the level of care your loved one needs and the room type you choose. Our Mount Vernon team will gladly walk you through a personalized quote and the funding options that may fit your situation. Call us and we'll give you a clear, no-pressure picture for your family.
Reviewed by Anneline Breetzke
Senior Care Specialist
Anneline Breetzke is a Senior Care Specialist who helps families navigate dementia, memory care, and the move from home to a community. This guide was written by the Whispering Willows Care Team and reviewed by Anneline for accuracy and tone against current guidance from Medicare.gov and the Washington State Health Care Authority.
Reviewed for accuracy · June 2026
A note on this guide. This article is educational and is not financial, legal, or medical advice. Benefit rules change over time and vary by individual circumstance. Please confirm your eligibility for any benefit with the VA, the Washington Health Care Authority (Apple Health), or a qualified benefits or elder-law professional before making decisions.
Sources
Medicare.gov. Long-term care. medicare.gov/coverage/long-term-care
Washington State Health Care Authority. Apple Health (Medicaid) coverage. hca.wa.gov/health-care-services-supports/apple-health-medicaid-coverage
